questions.question: A child is found to have bilateral meningiomas. This finding is most suggestive of which phakomatosis?
questions.detail: While bilateral acoustic schwannomas are the hallmark, multiple meningiomas in a child are a classic indicator of NF2, often remembered by the mnemonic MISME (multiple inherited schwannomas, meningiomas, and ependymomas).

questions.question: A 14-year-old gymnast presents with central low back pain and shooting pains in the legs. Radiographs and CT show a forward slip of L5 on S1. What is the most likely cause of this finding in this patient population?
questions.detail: Spondylolisthesis (the slip itself) in young athletes is most commonly secondary to bilateral spondylolysis (pars defects), which results from repetitive hyperextension.

questions.question: A routine examination of a 2-day-old baby reveals a palpable abdominal mass. Radiography shows a dense mass with multiple calcifications in the right lower quadrant. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Meconium peritonitis results from sterile bowel perforation in utero, leading to a calcified mass or scattered peritoneal calcifications identified at birth.

questions.question: A 3-year-old child with aniridia presents with a palpable renal mass that appears solid with cystic elements on ultrasound. No calcification is seen on CT. Diagnosis?
choices: E. Ossifying renal tumour of infancy
questions.detail: Aniridia is part of the WAGR syndrome (Wilms’ tumour, Aniridia, Genitourinary abnormalities, and mental Retardation), and Wilms' is the most common childhood renal tumor.

questions.question: Radiographs of an infant with rickets show bowing of the long bones and widened growth plates. Which finding specifically suggests hypophosphatasia as the cause?
choices: B. Sclerotic rim to the epiphyses
C. Lucent extensions into the metaphyses
questions.detail: Hypophosphatasia is characterized by rickets-like changes with the distinctive feature of 'punched-out' lucent defects extending from the growth plate into the metaphysis.

questions.question: A 6-year-old with a facial rash and seizures has subependymal nodules and cortical tubers on brain MRI. Renal ultrasound shows multiple bilateral hyperechogenic lesions. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: The clinical triad of seizures, learning disability, and adenoma sebaceum, combined with subependymal nodules and renal angiomyolipomas, is diagnostic of Tuberous Sclerosis.

questions.question: In a neonate with both arterial (UAC) and venous (UVC) umbilical catheters, which characteristic identifies the UAC?
choices: A. UAC is smaller in calibre
B. Tip of the UAC lies at T8 level
C. UAC courses down into the pelvis before approaching the thorax
D. UVC is longer than UAC
E. UAC tip always lies to the left of the UVC tip
questions.detail: The UAC enters the umbilical artery, descends into the internal iliac artery, and then ascends the aorta, giving it a characteristic pelvic 'dip' on radiographs.

questions.question: A girl is found to have a cystic RUQ mass. Ultrasound shows the cyst wall has an outer hypoechoic layer and an inner echogenic layer. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: This 'gut signature' (outer muscle layer and inner mucosal layer) is pathognomonic for an enteric duplication cyst.

questions.question: A baby boy has a distended bladder, a posterior urethral valve, bilateral megaureters, undescended testes, and widely separated rectus muscles. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Prune-belly (or Eagle–Barrett) syndrome consists of the triad of abdominal muscle deficiency, urinary tract abnormalities, and cryptorchidism.

questions.question: Which complication of neonatal meconium ileus is identified as a dense, calcified abdominal mass on birth radiographs?
questions.detail: Antenatal perforation due to meconium ileus (often associated with cystic fibrosis) leads to sterile meconium peritonitis and subsequent calcification.

questions.question: What is the most accurate ultrasound measurement for dating a pregnancy during the first trimester (up to 13 weeks)?
questions.detail: Crown–rump length (CRL) is the gold standard for dating in the first trimester, with an accuracy of approximately +/- 5 days.

questions.question: A 2-year-old presents with leukocoria. CT shows a hyperdense retrolental mass with punctate calcification and a dense vitreous. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Retinoblastoma is the most common intraocular tumor of childhood and characteristically contains calcification in 90% of cases.

questions.question: A newborn baby drools excessively and chokes during feeding. Radiographs show a coiled feeding tube in the proximal esophagus and gas in the stomach. Diagnosis?
choices: B. Oesophageal atresia and proximal tracheo-oesophageal fistula
C. Oesophageal atresia and distal tracheo-oesophageal fistula
D. Oesophageal atresia with both proximal and distal fistula
questions.detail: The presence of gas in the stomach confirms a distal fistula (the most common type, Type C), while the coiled tube confirms esophageal atresia.

questions.question: In which phakomatosis are subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs) typically found?
questions.detail: SEGAs are benign but potentially obstructing tumors found at the foramen of Monro in patients with Tuberous Sclerosis.

questions.question: What is the commonest cause of neonatal respiratory distress in premature infants, characterized by low lung volumes and granular opacities?
choices: A. Transient tachypnoea of the newborn
C. Respiratory distress syndrome (Hyaline membrane disease)
questions.detail: RDS is caused by surfactant deficiency in premature neonates, appearing as fine 'ground-glass' granularity and air bronchograms.

questions.question: A ventilation-perfusion scan in a patient with pulmonary hypoplasia is most likely to demonstrate which pattern?
choices: A. Matched marked reduction in ventilation and perfusion
B. More marked reduction in perfusion than ventilation
C. More marked reduction in ventilation than perfusion
D. Normal ventilation with reduced perfusion
E. Normal perfusion with reduced ventilation
questions.detail: Pulmonary hypoplasia involves a congenitally small lung with rudimentary vessels and bronchi, resulting in a matched defect.

questions.question: A 6-year-old girl has jaundice and a palpable mass. Ultrasound shows a cystic structure at the porta hepatis communicating with intrahepatic ducts. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: The triad of pain, jaundice, and a palpable mass is classic for a choledochal cyst (Type 1 being most common).

questions.question: A child with Kawasaki disease presents with RUQ pain. Ultrasound shows a markedly distended, thin-walled gallbladder without stones. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Acute gallbladder hydrops is a known transient complication of Kawasaki disease, presenting with gallbladder distension but no stones or wall thickening.

questions.question: Tubular filling defects 20 cm in length containing central barium-filled canals are seen in the small bowel of a child from south-east Asia. Organism?
questions.detail: Ascaris worms appear as long filling defects, and the central barium line represents the parasite's own digestive tract.

questions.question: Which of the following is characteristic of viral pneumonia in children compared to bacterial pneumonia?
choices: C. Peribronchial linear densities and interstitial pattern
questions.detail: Viral pneumonias typically affect the airways and peribronchial tissues, leading to interstitial markings rather than dense consolidation.

questions.question: A 10-year-old has RVH on ECG and a CXR showing a dilated left pulmonary artery with mural calcification. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Post-stenotic dilatation of the left pulmonary artery is a classic radiographic sign of valvular pulmonary stenosis.

questions.question: Ossifications within the epiphyseal cartilage of the medial distal femur and proximal tibia in a 4-year-old with leg deformity suggest which condition?
questions.detail: Dysplasia epiphysealis hemimelica (Trevor disease) involves osteochondroma-like overgrowths arising from the epiphysis.

questions.question: Which pattern of hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy occurs characteristically in the watershed areas of premature babies?
questions.detail: PVL is the hallmark of chronic partial asphyxia in the premature brain, occurring in the periventricular watershed zones.

questions.question: A neonate with a 4 cm, solid, non-calcified suprarenal mass. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Adrenal hemorrhage is common in neonates and appears as a solid mass that evolves into a cystic lesion, unlike neuroblastoma which is usually calcified.

questions.question: A 6-year-old elbow radiograph shows centers for the capitellum, radial head, and trochlea. Why is this concerning?
choices: A. The trochlea appears before the internal epicondyle
B. The internal epicondyle should appear before the trochlea
C. The radial head appears after the olecranon
D. This is normal for a 6-year-old
E. The external epicondyle is missing
questions.detail: Based on the CRITOE sequence, the Internal (medial) epicondyle must appear before the Trochlea. If 'trochlea' ossification is seen without a medial epicondyle, it is actually a displaced medial epicondyle fracture.

questions.question: What is the pathognomonic finding for a branchial cleft cyst on CT?
choices: B. Location at the angle of the mandible
C. 'Beak sign' pointing between the internal and external carotid arteries
questions.detail: A second branchial cleft cyst (the most common type) often shows a 'beak' of tissue extending between the carotid vessels.

questions.question: The normal position of the duodenojejunal (DJ) junction is located where?
choices: A. To the left of the left pedicle at the level of the duodenal bulb
B. To the left of the left pedicle below the level of the duodenal bulb
C. Overlying the vertebral body at the level of the duodenal bulb
D. To the right of the right pedicle below the level of the duodenal bulb
E. To the right of the right pedicle above the level of the duodenal bulb
questions.detail: Assessment of the DJ junction position is mandatory to exclude malrotation; it should be to the left of the spine and at the same level as the bulb.

questions.question: Contrast enters both ureters and reaches the calyces without urinary tract dilatation on a VCUG. What is the reflux grade?
questions.detail: Grade II reflux reaches the collecting system but does not cause any dilatation or blunting of the calyces.

questions.question: In the early stages of hyaline membrane disease, what is typically the first feature seen on CXR?
choices: C. Fine granularity in both lungs
questions.detail: The earliest sign is uniform, fine, granular (ground-glass) opacification of the lungs due to alveolar collapse.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of sudden-onset, severe, unilateral testicular pain in the prepubertal age group?
questions.detail: Testicular torsion is the most common acute scrotal emergency in children before puberty.

questions.question: Which method of intussusception reduction is considered optimal due to higher success rates and lower radiation doses?
questions.detail: Air reduction has a success rate of 70–90% and is faster than liquid-based methods, reducing overall radiation.

questions.question: Which of the following is associated with a 'double bubble' sign on neonate abdominal radiographs?
questions.detail: Duodenal atresia is the classic cause, but midgut volvulus can also present this way if the obstruction is at the duodenum.

questions.question: A child with precocious puberty has a 10 cm adrenal mass invading the kidney. No other disease is seen. What is the stage?
questions.detail: In adrenal cortical carcinoma, invasion of adjacent organs (like the kidney) classifies the disease as Stage IV.

questions.question: A newborn girl has a thin-walled, anechoic abdominopelvic cyst with a 'daughter cyst' visible on ultrasound. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Neonatal ovarian cysts are common due to maternal hormone stimulation; a 'daughter cyst' (small follicle) confirms the diagnosis.

questions.question: A paediatric skull radiograph shows generalized dense bone. Which feature suggests pyknodysostosis over osteopetrosis?
choices: C. Wormian bones and obtuse mandibular angle
questions.detail: Pyknodysostosis is distinguished by Wormian bones and the characteristic absence of the normal mandibular angle.

questions.question: If the urachus remains patent throughout its entire length, what is the resulting clinical finding?
questions.detail: A completely patent urachus allows urine to drain from the bladder directly through the umbilicus.

questions.question: Which of the following is a lethal skeletal dysplasia characterized by a 'telephone receiver' femur?
questions.detail: Thanatophoric dysplasia (Type 1) is a lethal condition with severely bowed 'telephone receiver' long bones.

questions.question: Why do immature skeletons often demonstrate greenstick or torus fractures instead of complete fractures?
questions.detail: The thick, active periosteum in children is much stronger than in adults and often remains intact on one side of a fracture.

questions.question: A 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' heart on CXR is a sign of which condition?
choices: B. Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (Supracardiac)
C. Transposition of the great vessels
questions.detail: In supracardiac TAPVR, the dilated vertical vein and SVC form the 'head' of the snowman above the heart.

questions.question: Which syndrome is defined by the association of supravalvular aortic stenosis and mental retardation?
questions.detail: Williams’ syndrome (elfin facies) is the classic association for supravalvular aortic stenosis.

questions.question: A fetus has a 12 mm renal pelvis at 35 weeks. What is the recommended postnatal management?
choices: C. Ultrasound within 24 hours of birth
questions.detail: A renal pelvis >10 mm antenatally requires early postnatal ultrasound (within 24 hours) to assess for significant obstruction.

questions.question: On hip ultrasound, an alpha angle of 48 degrees suggests which status?
questions.detail: An alpha angle of less than 60 degrees (specifically <50) indicates a shallow acetabulum and potential instability.

questions.question: A child with ALL and low platelets has sudden weakness and a normal CT brain. Best next step?
questions.detail: In a patient with profound thrombocytopenia, spontaneous hemorrhage must be excluded; GRE or SWI is most sensitive for small hemorrhages.

questions.question: A triangular mass with a rippled border in the right upper lobe of an 18-month-old child after pneumonia treatment is most likely what?
questions.detail: The 'sail sign' and 'wave sign' (rippled border from rib indentations) are classic features of the normal infant thymus.

questions.question: What is the commonest chest radiograph finding in Tetralogy of Fallot?
choices: C. Concavity at the level of the pulmonary trunk
questions.detail: The combination of an elevated apex (RVH) and pulmonary trunk concavity (reduced flow) creates the classic 'boot-shaped' heart.

questions.question: A 5-week-old has projectile vomiting. Ultrasound shows a pyloric canal 18 mm long and muscle 3 mm thick. Management?
questions.detail: Measurements of canal length >15-17 mm and muscle thickness >3 mm are diagnostic for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis.

questions.question: Which condition causes massive colonic distension in a neonate failing to pass meconium and is relieved by a water-soluble enema?
questions.detail: Meconium plug syndrome (or small left colon syndrome) is often seen in infants of diabetic mothers and is cured by the diagnostic enema.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of colonic polyps in children, typically presenting with rectal bleeding?
questions.detail: Juvenile polyps are hamartomatous and the most frequent polyps found in the pediatric population.

questions.question: The 'pancake flat' vertebral body (Vertebra Plana) in a child is a classic sign of which condition?
questions.detail: Vertebra plana in a child should immediately prompt consideration of Langerhans cell histiocytosis (EG).

questions.question: CT shows the large bowel on the left and the small bowel on the right. The SMA is to the right of the SMV. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Non-rotation is a type of malrotation where the entire small bowel stays on the right and the colon on the left.

questions.question: A girl with reduced pain/temperature sensation has 7 mm tonsillar ectopia and syringohydromyelia. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Chiari I is defined by tonsillar ectopia (>5 mm) and is frequently associated with syrinx formation.

questions.question: A 31-week fetus has hydrops, cardiac enlargement, and a cerebral median tubular cystic space with high-velocity flow. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: The high-flow vascular malformation leads to high-output cardiac failure and hydrops in the fetus.

questions.question: Which is the most common intracranial finding on CT in cases of non-accidental injury (NAI)?
questions.detail: Subdural hematomas (especially interhemispheric ones) are the most frequent brain injury seen in child abuse.

questions.question: A 12-year-old has large septated cystic collections around both kidneys causing scalloping of the renal outlines. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Renal lymphangiectasia is a rare condition where dilated lymphatics form perinephric and parapelvic cysts.

questions.question: A 15-year-old with renal failure and nerve deafness likely has which hereditary condition?
questions.detail: Alport’s syndrome is a hereditary nephritis associated with sensorineural hearing loss and ocular abnormalities.

questions.question: A 2-month-old has hyperlucency in the LUL with contralateral shift and compression of the LLL. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: CLE presents with hyperexpansion of a lobe (usually LUL) and progressive respiratory distress in early infancy.

questions.question: A [99mTc]-pertechnetate study shows a focal area of increased activity in the RLQ that parallels the stomach. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Pertechnetate is taken up by heterotopic gastric mucosa, which is present in symptomatic Meckel’s diverticula.

questions.question: A full-term baby born to a diabetic mother fails to pass meconium. Enema shows a transition at the splenic flexure. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Also known as small left colon syndrome, this is highly associated with maternal diabetes and resolves with the enema.

questions.question: Which phakomatosis is associated with multiple renal cysts, pancreatic cysts, and posterior fossa hemangioblastomas?
questions.detail: VHL is characterized by visceral cysts and vascular tumors of the CNS and retina.

questions.question: In a patient with cystic fibrosis, upper-lobe-predominant bronchiectasis is common. What is the classic appearance?
questions.detail: CF bronchiectasis features airway thickening (tram-tracks), dilatation (signet rings), and mucus plugging (finger-in-glove).

questions.question: A child with colicky pain and a purpuric rash on the legs has bowel wall thickening on CT. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: HSP is a systemic vasculitis that frequently involves the GI tract, presenting with hemorrhage and wall thickening.

questions.question: Which finding is part of the 'hypoperfusion complex' in a child following blunt abdominal trauma?
questions.detail: The shock bowel / hypoperfusion complex is a marker of severe hypovolaemia and poor prognosis.

questions.question: What is the most sensitive investigation for diagnosing pediatric discitis?
questions.detail: MRI is highly sensitive and can show marrow edema and disc changes long before they are visible on other modalities.

questions.question: Which feature helps distinguish Meconium Aspiration Syndrome (MAS) from RDS on a chest radiograph?
questions.detail: Unlike RDS, MAS occurs in full-term babies and features hyperinflation and patchy 'ropey' opacities.

questions.question: Flaring of the iliac wings, flat acetabular roofs, and decreased iliac angles (giving a low iliac index) are pelvic signs of?
questions.detail: The 'elephant ear' iliac wings and low iliac index are characteristic pelvic findings in Down’s syndrome.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of neonatal intestinal obstruction?
questions.detail: Small bowel atresia (ileal and jejunal) is the leading cause of neonatal obstruction.

questions.question: Where is the germinal matrix located in the developing brain (24-32 weeks)?
choices: B. Anterior to the caudothalamic groove
questions.detail: This highly vascular region is the site of germinal matrix hemorrhage in premature infants.

questions.question: Which skeletal finding is NOT typically seen in pediatric rickets?
choices: C. Widening of the growth plate
D. Narrowing of the growth plate
questions.detail: Rickets causes failure of mineralization, leading to a widened (not narrowed) growth plate and splayed, cupped metaphyses.

questions.question: What is the most common lead point/cause for intussusception in children?
questions.detail: In 90% of cases, pediatric intussusception is idiopathic and thought to be triggered by lymphoid tissue swelling following a viral illness.

questions.question: Mesial temporal sclerosis is best identified on MRI by which finding?
choices: B. Increased T2/FLAIR signal in the hippocampus
C. Loss of normal internal morphology
questions.detail: The combination of volume loss, high T2 signal, and loss of normal architectural interdigitations identifies MTS.

questions.question: Which structure is always absent or abnormal in all types of holoprosencephaly?
questions.detail: The absence of the septum pellucidum is a consistent feature and a sensitive indicator of midline cleavage failure.

questions.question: A neonate has hazy LUL opacification that becomes hyperlucent 2 months later with shift. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: CLE can present as a fluid-filled hazy lobe early on (failing to clear fetal lung fluid) before becoming air-filled and hyperlucent.

questions.question: An incidental finding of a small atrophic ovary with stippled calcification in a 13-year-old girl is likely what?
questions.detail: An 'amputated' ovary is the result of torsion that led to infarction and subsequent atrophy.

questions.question: Spontaneous neonatal pneumothorax is a known association for which condition?
questions.detail: A search for renal anomalies via ultrasound is recommended when a neonate has an otherwise unexplained pneumothorax.

questions.question: Which finding makes a Dandy-Walker malformation 'classic' rather than a 'variant'?
choices: B. Enlarged posterior fossa and high torcula
questions.detail: The 'classic' malformation includes vermian hypoplasia, a massively dilated 4th ventricle, and an expanded posterior fossa with an elevated torcula.

questions.question: Which fracture is considered pathognomonic for non-accidental injury (NAI)?
questions.detail: The metaphyseal corner fracture is caused by a unique shearing force and is highly specific for child abuse.

questions.question: In the context of NAI, which rib fracture location is most characteristic of abuse?
questions.detail: Posterior rib fractures (near the spine) result from forceful squeezing of the chest wall.

questions.question: Which paediatric tumor typically shows a 'dumbbell' shape as it crosses the cribriform plate?
questions.detail: Esthesioneuroblastoma arises from olfactory cells and classically has an intracranial and extracranial 'waist' at the cribriform plate.

questions.question: A neonate has granular opacities and air bronchograms on CXR but was born at 39 weeks. Most likely cause?
questions.detail: Group B Strep pneumonia can perfectly mimic the imaging features of RDS but occurs in full-term infants.

questions.question: No biliary clearance into the bowel after 24 hours on phenobarbital-augmented HIDA scan is diagnostic of?
questions.detail: Total absence of bowel activity despite good hepatic tracer uptake is the hallmark of biliary atresia.

questions.question: Which of the following skeletal locations is NOT a common site for avulsion fractures in children?
questions.detail: Radial head fractures are typically compressive; the others are all classic growing-apophysis avulsion sites.

questions.question: A 13-year-old male with severe epistaxis and a mass widening the pterygopalatine fossa has which condition?
questions.detail: JNA is a highly vascular tumor centered on the sphenopalatine foramen, occurring almost exclusively in male teenagers.

questions.question: What is the primary feeding vessel for a JNA?
questions.detail: The internal maxillary artery (a branch of the ECA) is the main blood supply and the target for pre-op embolization.

questions.question: Osteogenesis imperfecta is characterized by which radiographic finding?
choices: B. Exuberant callus formation and multiple fractures
questions.detail: Weak bones in OI lead to multiple fractures, bowing, and distinctive bulky (hyperplastic) callus formation.

questions.question: A 'starry sky' appearance on pediatric liver ultrasound (bright portal triads) suggests?
questions.detail: Acute hepatitis reduces liver echogenicity, making the normal portal triads stand out brightly.

questions.question: A 2-year-old boy with precocious puberty has a 10 cm heterogeneous liver mass with coarse calcifications. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Hepatoblastoma is associated with precocious puberty (due to HCG secretion) and is the most common primary liver tumor of childhood.

questions.question: Which of the following is NORMAL on neonatal renal ultrasound?
choices: A. Cortex more echogenic than the liver
B. Large, prominent, hypoechoic medullary pyramids
E. Dilation of the renal pelvis >10 mm
questions.detail: Neonatal kidneys are unique for their high cortical echogenicity, large pyramids, and persistent fetal lobulation.

questions.question: A finding of renal artery aneurysms in a child with hypertension favors a diagnosis of?
questions.detail: NF1 causes dysplasia of arterial walls, classically leading to orificial renal artery stenosis or aneurysms.

questions.question: Which cervical cyst is characteristically found in the midline, anywhere from the tongue base to the thyroid?
questions.detail: Thyroglossal duct cysts are the most common midline congenital neck masses.

questions.question: A rib-based inhomogeneous mass with a large intrathoracic component and rib destruction in a child is most likely?
questions.detail: An Askin tumor is a primitive neuroectodermal tumor (PNET) of the Ewing's family that arises from the chest wall.

questions.question: Which fracture in a non-ambulant infant is highly suggestive of NAI?
questions.detail: While spiral fractures are common in walking toddlers, they are highly suspicious in infants who cannot yet walk.

questions.question: A 'yo-yo' appearance on MRI of a thumb injury is diagnostic of?
questions.detail: A Stener lesion occurs when the UCL is torn and caught superficial to the adductor aponeurosis, preventing healing.

questions.question: What is the 'next step' when a Stener lesion is suspected based on imaging?
questions.detail: A Stener lesion will not heal with immobilization and requires surgery to reposition the ligament.

questions.question: Which midline posterior fossa tumor in a child is 'T1 bright' and 'fat-containing'?
questions.detail: Dermoids contain sebaceous material (fat) and hair, appearing T1 bright and hypodense on CT.

questions.question: A 4 mm, blind-ending, compressible, fluid-filled tubular structure in the RLQ of a child is likely?
questions.detail: A normal pediatric appendix is compressible and should measure less than 6 mm in diameter.

questions.question: A 16-year-old female with primary amenorrhoea and a history of imperforate anus has a distended uterus. Diagnosis?
questions.detail: Imperforate anus is associated with other VACTERL or genitourinary anomalies like a transverse vaginal septum, leading to trapped menstrual blood.

questions.question: What is the commonest site for a pediatric neuroblastoma to occur?
questions.detail: The adrenal gland is the site of origin in approximately 50% of neuroblastoma cases.

questions.question: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR) of the supracardiac type has which classic look?
questions.detail: The head of the 'snowman' is formed by the anomalous veins draining into the vertical vein and SVC.

questions.question: Acute inflammation and circumferential wall thickening of the caecum in a neutropenic child is termed?
questions.detail: Typhlitis (neutropenic enterocolitis) is a life-threatening infection of the caecum in immunocompromised patients.

questions.question: A child has a large, cystic liver mass with internal septations identified at birth. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Mesenchymal hamartoma is the classic large cystic 'benign' liver tumor of infancy/newborns.
